coredata.dc:title: A multifunctional carbon quantum dot overcoming the BBB for modulating amyloid aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Chaoren
Wang, Yixuan
Ma, Yandong
Liu, Hang
Tang, Selene
Li, Yansong
Shi, Jingming
Ding, Shichao
Lyu, Zhaoyuan
coredata.dc:description: The abnormal aggregation of β-amyloid (Aβ), the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and the presence of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) cause the prevention and treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD) extremely difficult. In this study, we report multifunctional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with red emission (RCQDs) for targeting Aβ42, modulating Aβ42 aggregation, overcoming the BBB, and scavenging ROS. RCQDs demonstrate exceptional targeting affinity for Aβ42 species, and exhibit high inhibition and disaggregation efficiencies against Aβ42 aggregates. Moreover, RCQDs possess outstanding scavenging abilities against the ROS in cells, thus resulting in the mitigation of cellular oxidative damages. RCQDs also exhibited strong penetration through the BBB in both in vitro and in vivo models, highlighting their potential for brain-targeted treatment. Further investigations using primary microglial cells confirm the anti-neuroinflammation properties of RCQDs. This study unprecedently demonstrates the therapeutic potential of unmodified pure CQDs for overcoming the BBB, targeting Aβ42 species, modulating Aβ42 aggregation and scavenging ROS, offering a promising candidate for the treatment of AD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Alzheimer's disease
β-amyloid
Reactive oxygen species
Blood-brain barrier

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibition and disaggregation of Aβ by RCQDs
The effect of RCQDs in scavenging ROS
Intracellular uptake and BBB permeability assays

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and particle size distribution diagram of RCQDs (The particle size distribution diagram obtained by Nano Measurer 1.2). (b) HRTEM image and lattice distance of RCQDs. (c) AFM image and height-profile analysis of RCQDs. (d) Excitation−emission matrix of RCQDs solution. (e) XPS wide-scan spectra of RCQDs. XPS survey spectrum, high-resolution C 1s (f), high-resolution O 1s (g), and high-resolution N 1s (h) spectrum of RCQDs.

(a) ThT fluorescence assay of Aβ42 (20 μM) in the absence or presence of RCQDs (5 or 10 μg mL−1). (b) Corresponding inhibition efficiency of RCQDs for Aβ42 fibrillation. (c) ThT fluorescence assay of Aβ42 fibrils (20 μM) in the absence or presence of RCQDs (5 or 10 μg mL−1). (d) Corresponding disaggregation efficiency of RCQDs for Aβ42 fibrils. (e) TEM images of Aβ42 monomers (20 μM) incubated in the absence and presence of RCQDs. Scale bars, 500 nm. (f) TEM images of Aβ42 fibrils (20 μM) incubated in the absence and presence of RCQDs. Scale bars, 500 nm. (g–h) The corresponding size distribution histogram of (e) and (f) results. (The size distribution diagram obtained by Nano Measurer 1.2).

(a) The CLSM images of control group, Aβ42 (20 μM) group, and Aβ42 treated with RCQDs group. Nuclei were stained with DAPI (Blue), Aβ42 were stained with 6E10 (Green), and the red FL signal comes from RCQDs. Scale bars, 100 μm. (b) The CLSM image and corresponding Pearson's correlation coefficient, and (c) the corresponding line series analysis of the CLSM image.

ROS scavenging activity of RCQDs in HT22 cells. (a) The CLSM images of HT22 cells. The ROS-positive cells show green fluorescence. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue fluorescence). Concentrations of RCQDs are in μg mL−1. Scale bars, 50 μm. (b) Quantitative analysis by flow cytometry.

Intracellular cellular uptake of RCQDs and BBB permeability activities assays of RCQDs. (a) Quantitative analysis by flow cytometry. bEnd.3 cells were co-incubated with different concentration of RCQDs (Including 0, 5, 25, 50, 100 μg mL−1) for 24 h. (b) The CLSM images of bEnd.3 cells treated with RCQDs (5 μg mL−1) for 0, 1, 12, and 24 h. The nuclei were stained with DAPI (blue fluorescence). Cytomembrane were stained with DiO (green fluorescence). Scale bars, 100 μm. (c) Fluorescence signals of RCQDs in the brain tissues of mice 24 h after tail vein injection of RCQDs and the corresponding fluorescence signal value (From Fig. 5c left for quantification in Fig. 5c right). Scale bars, 1 cm.

Neurotoxicity and neuroinflammation studies. (a) The apoptosis of HT22 cells incubated with different concentrations of RCQDs (in μg mL−1). (b) The viability of HT22 cells incubated with different concentrations of RCQDs. (c) Aβ42 and Aβ42/RCQDs mediated cytotoxicity of HT22 cells. LPS-induced neuroinflammation model (primary microglia): ELISA detection results of IL-6 (d) and TNF-α (e).

(a) Schematic representation for the synthesis of RCQDs and (b) Illustration of the mechanism of the effect of RCQDs on overcoming the BBB, targeting Aβ42, inhibiting Aβ42 aggregation and scavenging ROS.

head.ce:title: A multifunctional carbon quantum dot overcoming the BBB for modulating amyloid aggregation and scavenging reactive oxygen species

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Chaoren Yan and Yixuan Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abnormal aggregation of β-amyloid (Aβ), the accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), and the presence of the blood-brain barrier (BBB) cause the prevention and treatment of Alzheimer's disease (AD) extremely difficult. In this study, we report multifunctional carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with red emission (RCQDs) for targeting Aβ42, modulating Aβ42 aggregation, overcoming the BBB, and scavenging ROS. RCQDs demonstrate exceptional targeting affinity for Aβ42 species, and exhibit high inhibition and disaggregation efficiencies against Aβ42 aggregates. Moreover, RCQDs possess outstanding scavenging abilities against the ROS in cells, thus resulting in the mitigation of cellular oxidative damages. RCQDs also exhibited strong penetration through the BBB in both in vitro and in vivo models, highlighting their potential for brain-targeted treatment. Further investigations using primary microglial cells confirm the anti-neuroinflammation properties of RCQDs. This study unprecedently demonstrates the therapeutic potential of unmodified pure CQDs for overcoming the BBB, targeting Aβ42 species, modulating Aβ42 aggregation and scavenging ROS, offering a promising candidate for the treatment of AD.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alzheimer's disease (AD), commonly referred to as dementia, is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that induces patients' memory loss, cognitive impairment, and in severe cases, loss of motor function, imposing significant burdens on both families and society [1–6]. However, the prevalent drug development strategies have thus far been challenging in delivering effective drugs for AD treatment, especially owing to the existence of the blood-brain barrier (BBB).

The abnormal aggregation of β-amyloid (Aβ) and elevation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the brain are usually considered as the primary contributors to AD [7–13]. Aβ peptides, derived from amyloid precursor protein after sequential cleavages by β- and γ-secretases, exhibit a propensity to self-assemble into highly stable β-sheet-rich aggregates, thereby forming neurotoxic Aβ aggregates [14–18]. Besides, the overproduction of ROS damages the organelles of neurons and induces cellular apoptosis and necrosis, pivotal processes in inducing the pathogenesis of AD [10–12,19]. Most interestingly, related studies investigated that numerous ROS are generated during the formation of Aβ aggregates. These ROS are produced by the Fenton reaction involving Aβ species with metal ions during the self-assembly of Aβ [20]. The generated ROS and Aβ species can induce an overaccumulation of inflammatory cytokines, such as TNFα, further leading to microglia activation [10,21–23]. Once activated, these microglia will release additional ROS, further contributing to neuronal loss. It has to be mentioned that due to the existence of the BBB [24,25], the inhibition of Aβ and the clearance of ROS in brain are difficult to achieve. Therefore, considering the above-mentioned challenges, the inhibition of amyloid, clearance of ROS, and overcoming the BBB are crucial aspects in preventing and treating AD.

The emergence of nanomaterials provides a powerful tool for scavenging ROS, inhibiting amyloid aggregation and overcoming the BBB [26–42]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising Aβ inhibitor due to the simple preparation process, easy functionalization, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and adjustable optical properties [43,44]. The conjugated π electrons, surface functional groups, and surface charge of CQDs can interact with amino acid residues of Aβ via π-π stacking, hydrogen bond, and electrostatic interactions, posing the potential to modulate the on-pathway Aβ aggregation and cytotoxicity [45–47]. Notably, certain types of CQDs also exhibit the capability to scavenge ROS within physiological environments [48]. Furthermore, some related studies also indicate that CQDs can specifically inhibit Aβ aggregation and scavenge ROS under the condition of breaking through the BBB [27–30]. Considering the outstanding potential of CQDs materials in the treatment of AD, it is crucial to conduct comprehensive investigations into their use in AD applications.

In this work, we systematically report a type of multifunctional CQDs with red emission (RCQDs) for effective intervention of the causative factors of AD (Scheme 1 ). Experimental results have demonstrated that RCQDs are able to effectively target Aβ42 species and inhibit Aβ42 aggregation. In addition, the multifunctionality of RCQDs on scavenging ROS, blocking inflammatory factors, and inhibiting cytotoxicity was systematically investigated. Furthermore, through a series of in vitro and in vivo experiments, the RCQDs exhibited a remarkable ability to cross the BBB. Collectively, these results demonstrate the potency of RCQDs in relevant fields, and provide novel nanomaterials for research on the prevention and treatment of AD.

Based on the previously reported works with some slight modifications [49], RCQDs were synthesized using l-Glutamate and 1,2-diaminobenzene precursors via hydrothermal reaction. The morphologies of RCQDs were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscope (AFM). As shown in Fig. 1 a, the obtained RCQDs are uniformly dispersed with average diameters of RCQDs of 3.15 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) imaging result (Fig. 1b) indicates that the lattice fringes of RCQDs are identical to an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm, which corresponds the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [44]. AFM results (Fig. 1c) reveal that the heights of RCQDs are well in agreement with the TEM results, indicating that these nanomaterials exhibit a spherical morphology.

In addition, UV–vis absorption spectrum of RCQDs shown in Fig. S1 is thoroughly analyzed. The absorption peak at 240−300 nm is attributed to the π−π* transition of CC bonds in the sp2 core [50,51]. In addition, peaks at 500−600 nm were assigned to the n−π* transition of CO and CN on the surface of RCQDs [30,44]. The excitation−emission fluorescent matrix (Fig. 1d) of RCQDs showed excitation-independent emission, and the maximum excitation wavelength 530 nm and 660 nm, respectively. In addition, the absolute fluorescence quantum yields for RCQDs, when excited at the optimal wavelength, is about 16.9 % (As shown in Fig. S2).

In Fig. S3, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of RCQDs exhibited noticeable peaks at approximately 1517, 1621, 1638, 3295, and 3591 cm−1, corresponding to CC, CN, CO, N–H, and –O–H stretching vibrations, respectively, indicating the existence of carboxyl (−COOH), amino (−NH2), and hydroxyl (−OH) groups [29,44]. Additionally, the compositions of RCQDs are investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In Fig. 1e, the XPS wide-scan spectra display three typical peaks at 284.8, 400.6 and 532.1 eV for C1s, N1s, and O1s [51,52], respectively, indicating that RCQDs consisted of the C (26.4 %), N (9.4 %), and O (64.2 %) elements. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C and O (Fig. 1f and g) confirm the existence of CC/C–C (284.8 eV), C–O\C–N (286.1 eV), –CO (287.3 eV), –COOH (288.5 eV), C–O–C/C–O–H (530.6 eV) and CO (532.8 eV). The high-resolution XPS spectra of N (Fig. 1h) can be converted into N–H (397.3 eV) and C–N–C (399.1 eV) [29,44]. Based on the above data, hydroxyl, carboxyl and amidogen are the main functional groups on the surface of RCQDs. The presence of these functional groups establishes the working environment for interactions between RCQDs and Aβ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition and disaggregation of Aβ by RCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Aβ42 is considered to be the most neurotoxic amyloid peptide [53]. Therefore, we investigated the ability of RCQDs to inhibit Aβ42 fibrillation and disaggregate mature Aβ42 fibrils. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the effect of RCQDs in inhibiting Aβ42 fibrillization were explored by ThT assay in situ. The ThT fluorescence intensity of untreated Aβ42 changed sharply and reached the highest value at 3 h, and was essentially stable within the 3–36 h window. This data demonstrates the rapid aggregation of Aβ42 in the first 8 h, and the formation of Aβ42 fibrils by 36 h. Subsequently, the morphology of Aβ42 is investigated by TEM to confirm the above observations. As shown in Fig. 2e (control group), Aβ42 alone aggregated into densely tangled fibrils, similar to previous experimental results [29]. With the addition of RCQDs, the ThT fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced (Fig. 2a), and the Aβ42 was unable to form small spherical, irregular aggregates, and fibrils (Fig. 2e, RCQDs group). The added RCQDs exhibited an inhibition efficiency of over 91 % (Fig. 2b), indicating the outstanding capabilities of RCQDs in inhibiting amyloid aggregation. In addition, the corresponding statistical length of the control group and the RCQDs group were 830 and 60 nm (Fig. 2g), respectively, demonstrating the considerable active duration of RCQDs in suppressing the mature Aβ42 fibrils formation (36 h). In addition to inhibition efficiency, the disaggregation capacity of RCQDs was also investigated. The Aβ42 monomers were incubated for 24 h and the ThT kinetics assay of Aβ42 fibrillation was performed. At 24 h, RCQDs were added into the above samples while being continually incubated. As shown in Fig. 2c, the prepared Aβ42 fibrils can be effectively disaggregated by RCQDs, and the results exhibited an impressive disaggregation efficiency of over 90 % (Fig. 2d). The results of TEM images indicate that RCQDs are capable of inducing the dissociation of Aβ42 fibrils into minimal fragments (Fig. 2f). These TEM images also showed a significant mean length difference of control group and RCQDs group, which were 534 nm and 78 nm, respectively (Fig. 2h).

One of the major causes of Aβ42-mediated neurotoxicity is the destruction of the plasma membrane and other organelles by Aβ42 fibrillation. The inhibitory effect of Aβ42 aggregation at the mouse hippocampal neuronal cell line (HT22 cell) was thus evaluated by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) for optimal resolution and contrast. As shown in Fig. S4, HT22 cells incubated with FITC labeled Aβ42 (FITC-Aβ42) for 24 h exhibited significant staining with Aβ42 species (encompassing both monomers and aggregates) compared to the control group. This result demonstrates the strong binding affinity of Aβ42 species to cells. In addition, the binding of Aβ42 species to the cell also induces alterations in cell membrane permeability and metabolic disorders. Notably, when HT22 cells are treated with FITC-Aβ42 and RCQDs for 24 h, a significant decrease of the fluorescence signal of FITC-Aβ42 was observed (Fig. S4). Furthermore, the corresponding fluorescence intensity also directly illustrated the change mentioned above (Fig. S4). ThT labeling experiments also demonstrated that RCQDs can inhibit the Aβ42 aggregates attached to HT22 cells. As shown in Fig. S5, HT22 cells incubated with Aβ42 exhibited significant ThT fluorescence levels, indicating the presence of large quantities of Aβ42 aggregates surrounding the HT22 cells. In contrast, HT22 cells incubated with Aβ42 and RCQDs showed substantially lower fluorescence signal, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity of ThT was significantly reduced in comparison to the Aβ42 group in absence of RCQDs (Fig. S5).

Based on the experimental results and the analysis of the previous section, RCQDs have exhibited excellent inhibition and disaggregation ability. These outstanding properties of RCQDs can be attributed to their targeted affinity for Aβ42. To verify this hypothesis, the binding interactions between RCQDs and Aβ42 species were investigated using HT22 cells in vitro. The HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 were initially stained with RCQDs and then counterstained with 6E10, a type of Aβ-specific antibodies. 6E10 can react with amino acid residue 1–16 of Aβ to recognize the N terminus of human Aβ and bind to the monomer, parenchymal plaques [54,55]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the CLSM images of HT22 cells revealed that 6E10-positive Aβ42 species were substantially colocalized with RCQDs. The Pearson's correlation coefficient (Rr = 0.85) of 6E10-positive Aβ42 species/RCQDs (Fig. 3b) and the corresponding line series analysis (Fig. 3c) further verified their strong colocalization. In addition, to further demonstrate the direct binding between Aβ42 and RCQDs, the FL intensity of tyrosine in Aβ42 were measured after treatment with different concentration of RCQDs [56]. As shown in Fig. S6, the FL emission spectra depict Aβ42 alone and Aβ42 co-incubated with RCQDs. The tyrosine FL intensity decreased significantly after the addition of RCQDs compared to Aβ42 alone. With the increase of the concentration (5, 10, 25, 50, 100 μg mL−1) of RCQDs, the FL intensity of tyrosine decreased by about 37.7, 60.8, 90.7, 99.1 and 99.7 %, respectively. Moreover, the emission peak of tyrosine exhibited a red shift from 305 nm to 307 nm. These results highlighted the Aβ42 species-binding affinity ability of RCQDs, revealing their potential for targeted elimination and visual imaging of Aβ42 species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of RCQDs in scavenging ROS
ce:section.ce:para: Overproduction of ROS can lead to neurological dysfunction and induction of cellular apoptosis and necrosis, contributing crucially to the pathogenesis of AD [10]. Consequently, the removal of excess ROS could serve as an effective treatment for AD. Intracellular ROS scavenging activity of RCQDs was evaluated with HT22 cells by DCFH-DA assay. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 displayed clear green fluorescence compared to the control group. After the addition of RCQDs, the green fluorescence intensity was significantly reduced, and the green fluorescence gradually disappeared with the increase in the concentration of RCQDs. In addition, the changes of intracellular total ROS mediated by Aβ42 were quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry (Fig. 4b). In contrast to the empty control group, the ROS concentration of HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 exhibited a significant increase of 91.5 %. After 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1 RCQDs treatment, the level of ROS was reduced to 69.7, 33.8, 13.9, 7.05 and 3.89 %, respectively. For further examining the ROS scavenging activity of the as-prepared RCQDs, DPPH was also selected as the model radical. As an N-centered free radical, DPPH exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 515 nm. When DPPH was in contact with free radical scavengers, the peak decreases due to the formation of the DPPH complex [57]. As shown in Fig. S7, the peak intensity of DPPH at 515 nm continuously declined as the concentration of RCQDs increased from 0 to 100 μg mL−1. The scavenging efficiency of RCQDs for DPPH free radicals increased with the concentration of RCQDs. To be specific, the efficiency of RCQDs reached 85 % at 100 μg mL−1. This further suggested that RCQDs possess a remarkable capacity to scavenge ROS. Overall, these results collectively demonstrate that RCQDs can effectively eliminate intracellular ROS and further highlight their potential as excellent ROS scavenger.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular uptake and BBB permeability assays
ce:section.ce:para: The prevention and treatment of AD is extremely limited due to the existence of the BBB. The brain microvascular endothelial cells play a crucial role in the composition of the BBB. Therefore, the mouse brain microvascular endothelial cells (bEnd.3 cells) were selected to investigate the intracellular uptake of RCQDs. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after 24 h co-incubation with different concentration of RCQDs (5, 25, 50, 100 μg mL-1), the intracellular uptake of RCQDs by bEnd.3 cells was noticeable compared to the control group. After 5, 25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1 RCQDs treatment, the intracellular uptake of RCQDs was decreased to 6.58, 14.9, 18.8, and 20.6 %, respectively. In addition, the intracellular uptake of RCQDs in bEnd.3 cells was also measured using CLSM. As shown in Fig. 5b, bEnd.3 cells in the RCQDs-treated group were marked by red fluorescence, in contrast to the control group, with the intensity of the red fluorescence in bEnd.3 cells increased over the incubation time (Fig. S8). These results indicated that RCQDs could be endocytosed by bEnd.3 cells. Subsequently, the Transwell assay was conducted to evaluate the BBB permeability of RCQDs (Fig. S9a). After adding RCQDs in apical chamber and incubating for 24 h, the uptake of RCQDs by HT22 cells in basal chamber reached 99.6 % (Fig. S9b). Meanwhile, the fluorescence signal of HT22 cells in basal chamber was stronger than that of the control group (Figs. S9c and d). After 24 h incubation, the transport ratios were calculated to be 43.8 % (Figs. S9e and f). These results suggested that RCQDs had a greater ability to penetrate the BBB. Moreover, RCQDs were injected into mice by tail vein to investigate the BBB crossing capability of this nanomaterials. The obtained brain tissue was investigated through Ex vivo fluorescence imaging assay. As shown in Fig. 5c, brain tissues extracted from the mice treated with RCQDs exhibited noticeable fluorescence signals compared to the control group. These comparable results through Ex vivo fluorescence imaging assay also indicated that RCQDs have great potential in crossing the BBB.

RCQDs’ cytotoxicity and biocompatibility must be determined when applied in vitro and in vivo. As shown in Fig. 6 a, Annexin V-FITC/PI kit was utilized to detect the apoptosis caused by RCQDs. The results showed that no noticeable apoptosis phenomenon was observed after treatment of RCQDs at various concentrations. The CCK-8 assay was utilized to determine the effect of RCQDs at different concentrations on the viability of HT22 cells. As shown in Fig. 6b, when the concentration of RCQDs was 200 μg mL−1, the survival rate of HT22 cells was greater than 90 % while that of 300 μg mL−1 group was less than 70 %. These data indicated that RCQDs have low cytotoxicity under the experimentally determined concentration threshold of approximately 300 μg mL−1. To evaluate the anti-Aβ toxicity of RCQDs, the viability of HT22 cells treated with Aβ42 only and Aβ42 with RCQDs was also examined. As shown in Fig. 6c, Aβ42 alone exhibited significant toxicity with considerably low cell viability of 80 %. After 24 h of various treatments, it was confirmed that the addition of RCQDs effectively suppressed the toxicity of Aβ42. Namely, the RCQDs treated groups showed a remarkably higher survival rate of greater than 92 % at 5 μg mL−1. Collectively, results in Fig. 6c demonstrated that RCQDs exhibited a notable neuroprotective effect against Aβ-induced cytotoxicity. To further investigate the biocompatibility of RCQDs, normal mice were treated with RCQDs. The treatment of RCQDs yielded no abnormal behavior and the main organs (Heart, Liver, Spleen, Lung, Kidney) of the mice were harvested for further histopathological analysis. As shown in Fig. S10, the absence of pathological abnormalities indicated that RCQDs possess favorable biocompatibility, making them promising candidates for further applications in AD treatment.

Studies have showed that the activated microglia can generated ROS and lead to neuronal loss [58]. Primary microglia were induced by LPS, establishing a neuroinflammatory model to detect whether RCQDs could inhibit the expression of inflammatory factors. As shown in Fig. 6d and e, the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-α and IL-6 increased significantly after LPS stimulation. After 24 h of treatment, RCQDs led to the significant elimination of TNF-α and IL-6, indicating that RCQDs has a good inhibitory effect on neuroinflammation.

In summary, a multifunctional red fluorescent CQDs was fabricated via hydrothermal reaction using l-Glutamate and 1,2-diaminobenzene precursors. The prepared RCQDs efficiently target Aβ42 species, as demonstrated by their Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.85, which verifies their strong colocalization. In addition, RCQDs exhibited effective blocking abilities against the Aβ42 fibrillation, and significant disaggregating capabilities against the prepared Aβ42 aggregates. Notably, the inhibition and disaggregation efficiency of RCQDs reach 91.6 and 90.3 %. Moreover, the versatility of RCQDs is also reflected in their strong ROS scavenging properties, blocking neuroinflammation and inhibiting Aβ42 induced cytotoxicity. Finally, the RCQDs were shown to cross the BBB and effectively enriched in the brain through experiments using both in vitro and in vivo models. Taken together, RCQDs poses promising potential in future exploration of the possibilities in overcoming BBB and effectively treating AD.

Chaoren Yan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yixuan Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yandong Ma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Hang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Selene Tang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yansong Li: Software, Resources, Formal analysis. Jingming Shi: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Shichao Ding: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis. Zhaoyuan Lyu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors from Xizang Minzu University thank the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82360368), Youth Project of the Natural Science Foundation of Tibet Autonomous Region (Grant No. XZ202401ZR0078), Innovation Capability Support Plan of Shaanxi Province (Grant No. 2020TD-041), Young Talent Funding Project of Xizang Minzu University (Grant No. 23MDX03).
